deals_cleared.merger_description: Eli Lilly and Company (**Eli Lilly**) proposes to acquire 100% of the issued shares in Orna Therapeutics Holdings, LLC (**Orna Therapeutics**) (the **Acquisition**).

events.title: Eli Lilly - Orna Therapeutics - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Philip Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Eli Lilly and Company (Eli Lilly) proposes to acquire 100% of the
issued shares in Orna Therapeutics Holdings, LLC (Orna
Therapeutics) (together, the Parties) (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Eli Lilly, is a US-based pharmaceutical company, which
discovers, develops, manufactures and markets human
pharmaceutical products for various therapeutic areas, including
diabetes, oncology, immunology and neuroscience. Eli Lilly is
publicly listed on the New York Stock Exchange.
The target, Orna Therapeutics is a US-based pre-clinical
biotechnology company developing a new class of engineered
circular RNA therapeutics and immune tropic lipid nanoparticles
(LNP) delivery technologies for the treatment of autoimmune
disorders, and cancer conditions. Orna Therapeutics has a pipeline
consisting of two in vivo Chimeric Antigen Receptor T-cell (CAR-T)
therapies, designed to treat B cell-driven autoimmune diseases.
Orna Therapeutics currently has existing licensing arrangements to
supply use of its immune tropic LNP delivery technology to Vertex,
and its engineered circular RNA technology to Merck.

There is no overlap in existing products supplied by the Parties. The
ACCC has considered potential overlap in therapies in the early
stages of the research pipeline.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form and engagement with third parties.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• Post-acquisition, the merged firm would continue to face
competition from alternative suppliers developing relevant
therapies in the early stages of development.
• Post-acquisition, the merged firm would be unlikely to have the
ability and incentive to foreclose Vertex and/or Merck’s access
to inputs given there are alternative suppliers of immune tropic
LNP delivery technology and engineered circular RNA
technology.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

(**Estia**) proposes to acquire substantially all of the business assets of three residential aged care homes from Norsan Pty Ltd (**Norsan**), being Chirnside Views Residential Aged Care, Park Lane Croydon Residential Aged Care and Willowbrae - Melton Residential Aged Care (the **Acquisition**).

events.title: Phase 1 Determination - Estia Health - Norsan residential aged care business
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Estia Investments Pty. Ltd. (Estia) proposes to acquire substantially
all of the business assets of three residential aged care homes from
Norsan Pty Ltd (Norsan), being Chirnside Views Residential Aged
Care (Chirnside Views), Park Lane Croydon Residential Aged Care
(Park Lane Croydon) and Willowbrae - Melton Residential Aged Care
(Willowbrae – Melton) (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

Estia is a registered provider of residential care services under
the Aged Care Act 2024 (Cth) (ACA). Estia and its wholly owned
subsidiaries currently operate over 90 residential aged care homes
across Victoria, New South Wales, Queensland, and South Australia.
Norsan is a small family-owned supplier of three residential aged
care services operating in certain parts of metropolitan Melbourne
and outer Melbourne suburbs. Norsan is also a registered provider
of residential care services under the ACA.

Estia and Norsan overlap in the supply of residential aged care
facilities in Melbourne.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form and information from third partes and publicly
available information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• The aggregation arising from the Acquisition, and taking into
account previous acquisitions by Estia within the same local
area as the Norsan facilities in the last three years, is not
significant at a local or regional level (nor at the national level).
• Estia will likely continue to face competition from alternative
suppliers of residential aged care facilities in the local areas
surrounding each of Chirnside Views, Park Lane Croydon and
Willowbrae – Melton.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Dubai Aerospace Enterprise Ltd (**DAE**) proposes to acquire 100% of the issued share capital in Macquarie AirFinance Limited (**Macquarie AirFinance**) (the **Acquisition**).

events.title: Questionnaire - Dubai Aerospace Enterprise - Macquarie AirFinance
events.display_title: Questionnaire - Dubai Aerospace Enterprise - Macquarie AirFinance

events.title: Phase 1 Determination - Dubai Aerospace Enterprise - Macquarie AirFinance
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Dubai Aerospace Enterprise (DAE) Ltd (DAE) proposes to acquire
100% of the issued share capital in Macquarie AirFinance Limited
(Macquarie AirFinance) (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

DAE is a globally active aircraft lessor, headquartered in Dubai. DAE
is active in leasing regional, narrow-body and wide-body aircraft to
airlines. DAE consists of two divisions: DAE Capital and DAE
Engineering. DAE Capital carries out aircraft leasing, aircraft trading
and provides aircraft investor services. DAE Engineering, through its
subsidiary Joramco, provides regional maintenance repair and
overhaul services.
Macquarie AirFinance is a globally active aircraft lessor of civil
aviation aircraft, headquartered in Dublin. Macquarie AirFinance is
primarily active in leasing narrow-body aircraft, and, to a lesser
extent, wide-body aircraft to airlines.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
DAE and Macquarie AirFinance overlap in the supply of aircraft dry
leases to airlines.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, publicly available information, and feedback from
market participants.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• Aggregation in the supply of aircraft dry leases resulting from
the Acquisition appears low globally, including into Australia,
and the merged entity would likely continue to be constrained by
alternative suppliers of aircraft dry leases post-acquisition.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Host-Plus Pty. Limited (**Hostplus**) proposes to acquire 50% of CH Investment Holding Trust (**CH Holding Trust**), a Charter Hall managed vehicle (the **Acquisition**).

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Host-Plus Pty. Limited as trustee for the Hostplus Pooled
Superannuation Trust (Hostplus) applied for a notification waiver in
respect of its proposed acquisition of 50% of CH Investment Holding
Trust (CH Holding Trust), as described in the transaction documents
provided as part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, Hostplus, is the trustee for each of the Hostplus Pooled
Superannuation Trust and the Hostplus Superannuation Fund. The
Hostplus Pooled Superannuation Trust is the unit trust vehicle that
the Hostplus Superannuation Fund utilises to access investments for
its members. The Hostplus Superannuation Fund has 1.87 million
members (as at 30 June 2025), with current funds under
management of $134 billion (as at 30 June 2025). Hostplus invests
across a diversified portfolio of asset classes, including equities,
fixed income, infrastructure, property, private equity and alternative
investments, for the benefit of its members. Hostplus has passive
minority investments in funds that have exposure to commercial
office space in the Sydney CBD.
The target, CH Holding Trust, indirectly holds commercial property
assets in the Sydney CBD. CH Holding Trust is managed by Charter
Hall which is an Australian property investment and funds
management group, that acts as a fiduciary for wholesale,
institutional, and retail investors.
Through the Acquisition, Hostplus will gain a 50% indirect interest in
the commercial property assets indirectly owned by CH Holding
Trust in the Sydney CBD.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application, and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
a. the increment to Hostplus’ interests in commercial office
property in Sydney CBD will be small
b. there are alternative commercial office offerings in the
Sydney CBD.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Hudson Highland (APAC) Pty Ltd (**HHA**) is the holding company of Hudson Global Resources (Aust) Pty Ltd (**Hudson Global Resources (Aust)**), which provides recruitment and labour hire services in Australia.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Oceania Capital Partners Limited (Oceania Capital Partners) applied
for a notification waiver in respect of its and K & K Ryan Pty Ltd’s
proposed acquisition of 33.15% of the share capital each in Hudson
Highland (APAC) Pty Ltd, the holding company of Hudson Global
Resources (Aust) Pty Ltd (Hudson Global Resources (Aust)), as
described in the transaction documents provided as part of the
application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

Oceania Capital Partners is a Sydney based unlisted public company
that pursues private equity style investments in businesses. This
includes interests in home security, aerospace, broadcasting and
health, among others.
K & K Ryan Pty Ltd as trustee for the K & K Ryan Family Trust is a
private investor associated with Hudson Global Resources (Aust)’s
Chief Financial Officer, Kendall Ryan.
Hudson Global Resources (Aust) is an Australian-owned recruitment
and talent management agency, operating since 1985 with offices
across all major Australian states and territories. They specialize in
permanent, contract, and high-volume recruitment for sectors
including government, tech, engineering, and finance, placing roughly
5,600 on-hire staff annually.
Oceania Capital Partners and K & K Ryan Pty Ltd do not have any
other investments in recruitment and labour hire businesses, or
investments in related businesses at different levels in the supply
chain for recruitment and labour hire in Australia.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular, there is no horizontal overlap
or vertical relationships between Oceania Capital Partners, K & K
Ryan Pty Ltd and Hudson Global Resources (Aust) in the supply of
recruitment and labour hire services in Australia.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_name: Eagers - Audi Melbourne and Audi Richmond
deals_cleared.merger_description: Eagers Vic Pty Ltd (Eagers) is a subsidiary of Eagers Automotive Limited (ASX: APE), a publicly listed automotive retail group which owns and operates motor vehicle dealerships across Australia. Eagers Automotive Limited sells new and used motor vehicles through its dealerships, representing around 50 automotive brands. Eagers Automotive Limited also provides related goods and services including motor vehicle service, parts and allied consumer finance. In Victoria, Eagers has 29 dealerships, 12 of which supply automotive brands that offer similar prices and features to Audi (including but not limited to Mercedes-Benz, BMW, Volvo, Land Rover, Volkswagen). Eagers does not currently supply Audi vehicles.

events.title: Questionnaire - Eagers - Audi Melbourne and Audi Richmond
events.display_title: Questionnaire - Eagers - Audi Melbourne and Audi Richmond

events.title: Eagers - Audi Melbourne and Audi Richmond - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Eagers Vic Pty Ltd’s (Eagers) proposed acquisition of two Audi
dealerships, as described below, from Zagame Automotive Group
Pty Ltd (Zagame) (together, the Parties).
The Audi dealerships are:
• Audi Centre Melbourne, 501 Swanston Street, Melbourne
3000 (Audi Melbourne)
• Audi Richmond, 408-410 Swan Street, Richmond 3121 (Audi
Richmond)
(the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Eagers, is a subsidiary of Eagers Automotive Limited.
Eagers Automotive Limited operates dealerships which supply new
and used motor vehicles, with a portfolio of approximately 50
automotive brands. Eagers Automotive Limited operates in each
state and territory of Australia, including as Eagers in Victoria. In
Victoria, Eagers has 29 dealerships, 12 of which supply automotive
brands that offer similar prices and features to Audi (including but
not limited to Mercedes-Benz, BMW, Volvo, Land Rover,
Volkswagen). Eagers does not currently supply Audi vehicles.
The targets, Audi Melbourne and Audi Richmond, are currently
owned and operated by Zagame. Zagame operates dealerships in
the Melbourne area and in Adelaide, with a portfolio of 16
automotive brands. In addition to Audi Melbourne and Audi
Richmond, Zagame owns and operates three further Audi
dealerships in Brighton, Mornington and Essendon Fields. Audi
Melbourne and Audi Richmond do not supply used vehicles.

Horizontal overlap in the retail supply of new cars in greater
Melbourne.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, information from prior matters, and publicly
available information
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• There is no horizontal overlap between the Parties in relation to
the supply of Audi vehicles in greater Melbourne.
• In relation to the broader supply of new vehicles in greater
Melbourne, and more narrowly in relation to the supply of
automotive brands that offer similar prices and features to Audi,
the increase in Eagers’ share of supply is expected to be small.
• Eagers would likely continue to compete with a large number of
alternative automotive dealerships in greater Melbourne. This
includes seven alternative Audi dealerships in greater
Melbourne.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Danaher Corporation (Danaher) proposes to acquire 100% of the outstanding share capital of Masimo Corporation (Masimo).

events.title: Danaher - Masimo - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Danaher Corporation (Danaher) proposes to acquire 100% of the
outstanding share capital of Masimo Corporation (Masimo) (the
Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Danaher, is a global science and technology company.
Danaher designs, manufactures and markets professional, medical,
industrial and commercial products and services under three core
segments: biotechnology, life sciences, and diagnostics.
The target, Masimo, Masimo is a global medical technology
company. Masimo is a provider of pulse oximetry and other patient
sensors and monitoring solutions.

The parties both supply patient monitor products. Further, Danaher
supplies a product called transcutaneous monitors (TCMs), certain
of which incorporate Masimo’s pulse oximetry sensors.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form and information from third partes.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• There is limited horizontal overlap between Danaher and
Masimo’s products. Danaher’s patient monitors are primarily
used in ICU and NICU settings, while Masimo’s patient monitors
are primarily used in general wards.
• Danaher would likely continue to be constrained by alternative
suppliers of patient monitoring technology by Medtronic, Philips,
MindRay, GE Healthcare and Sentec.
• Danaher is unlikely to have the ability to foreclose downstream
rivals supplying TCMs in Australia from access to pulse oximetry
sensor technology following the Acquisition, given there are
alternative suppliers of pulse oximetry technology (Medtronic,
Philips, Mindray, Sentec).

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

events.title: Questionnaire - Journey Beyond - Kakadu Crocodile Hotel
events.display_title: Questionnaire - Journey Beyond - Kakadu Crocodile Hotel

events.title: Journey Beyond - Kakadu Crocodile Hotel - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Experience Australia Group Pty Ltd (EAG)’s proposed acquisition, via
its wholly owned subsidiary JB Kakadu Hotel Pty Ltd
(JB Kakadu), of Crocodile Hotel Pty Ltd, which owns the business
and business assets known as the Kakadu Crocodile Hotel
(Crocodile Hotel) in Jabiru, NT, as well as a 100% leasehold interest
in the land on which the Crocodile Hotel is situated from Gundjeihmi
Aboriginal Corporation Jabiru Town (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, EAG, primarily supplies tourism services and multi-
destination packaged tours in Australia under the brand name
“Journey Beyond”. Via its wholly owned subsidiary Outback Spirit
Tours Pty Ltd (Outback Spirit), EAG also supplies touring packages
to remote destinations across Western Australia, the Northern
Territory, Queensland and South Australia.
The target, Crocodile Hotel, is a 110-room hotel located in the
township of Jabiru in Kakadu National Park. In addition to
accommodation, its facilities include a restaurant, bar and an on-site
art gallery featuring local artwork.

EAG and the Crocodile Hotel both operate in the tourism services
supply chain in Kakadu. EAG currently uses accommodation
provided by the Crocodile Hotel on certain tours.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, information from third parties and publicly
available information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• There is no horizontal overlap between Journey Beyond and
Crocodile Hotel.
• While some market participants suggested that the Acquisition
could enable the Parties to foreclose rival tour providers’ access

events.title: REA Group - Simplicity Loans & Advisory - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by a division of the Commission constituted by a direction issued pursuant to section 19 of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
REA Financial Services Holding Co. Pty Ltd, as a subsidiary of REA
Group Ltd (REA Group) (ASX:REA), proposes to acquire 70% of the
issued share capital in Simplicity Loans & Advisory Pty Ltd
(Simplicity) (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, REA Group, provides digital advertising and mortgage
brokerage services through the below brands and platforms. News
Corp owns a ~61% controlling stake in REA Group.
REA Group owns and operates:
• Mortgage Choice Pty Ltd and Smartline Operations Pty
Ltd (Mortgage Choice), a mortgage broker which
specialises in residential mortgage broking services
(homes and personal loans) and also provides financial
planning services. Mortgage Choice also acts as an
aggregator – it connects brokers seeking residential
loans to a panel of lenders.
• Simpology (32.97% interest held by REA Group), an e-
lodgement platform used by brokers to submit loans to
a range of lenders.
• Athena Home Loans (19.9% interest held by REA Group),
a non-bank lender which has focuses on residential
home loans.
• Online property advertising platforms: realestate.com.au
(residential property), realcommercial.com.au
(commercial property), flatmates.com.au (share
accommodation), Spacely (short-term commercial and
co-working property), PropTrack (property data services
and market insights) and 1Form (centralised platform
for rental property applications).
The target, Simplicity, is a boutique mortgage broker operating in
Sydney, Melbourne and Brisbane specialising in commercial and
developer mortgages.
Simplicity also operates Marketplace Finance platform (MKP), a
bilateral mortgage referral tool connecting third-party referrers
(typically residential mortgage brokers with no commercial
experience) with Simplicity’s commercial mortage brokers.
Fulfilment of the loans occurs by Simplicity’s employed brokers via
aggregator panels or direct lender channels.
The reasons for the ACCC’s determination are set out in section 2.

determination_statement_of_reasons.text: When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines and interim merger process guidelines.
For the reasons given below, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into effect.
Industry background
Mortgage broking services form part of the broader mortgage distribution supply chains shown in Figure 1 below: Figure 1: Mortgage distribution supply chain1

items: Lenders provide finance to borrowers seeking to buy property. Lenders in Australia include the ‘big 4’ banks, other banks with an Australian presence2 and non-banks (e.g. La Trobe Financial, Pepper Money). Lenders can distribute their loans either directly or indirectly through mortgage brokers.

Mortgage aggregators such as Mortgage Choice, LMG, and AFG act as an intermediary between brokers and lenders, providing brokers with access to a panel of lenders and a range of mortgage products, which they in turn recommend to customers. Aggregators have contractual agreements with the lenders on their panel, which allows their affiliated brokers to arrange loans from those lenders for their customers.

Mortgage brokers, including Simplicity and the Mortgage Choice broker network act as an intermediary by connecting borrowers to lenders, generally via 1 ACCC, ‘AFG Connective - Statement Of Issues - 12 February 2020’, ACCC, 12 Feb 2020, accessed 29 April 2026. 2 For example, ING Bank, Bendigo and Adelaide Bank, Suncorp Bank and Macquarie Bank. aggregators. Mortgage brokers help customers select an appropriate loan, prepare the loan application, negotiate with lenders and settle the loan. Mortgage brokers are paid by lenders for the services they provide to consumers.

determination_statement_of_reasons.text: Overlap and relationship between the merger parties
The merger parties overlap in the supply of mortgage broking services in Australia:

items: Mortgage Choice’s core business is residential mortgage broking with a small number of commercial settlements via its national network of franchisees and credit representatives.
Simplicity is a boutique mortgage broker operating in Sydney, Melbourne and Brisbane specialising in commercial and developer mortgages.

determination_statement_of_reasons.text: The merger parties may also have a non-horizontal relationship where REA Group through Mortgage Choice brokers or via its online real estate advertising platforms may refer commercial borrower leads to Simplicity’s commercial mortgage brokers.
Competition assessment

The ACCC has considered the effects of the Acquisition by comparing the likely future state of competition if the Acquisition proceeds against the continuation of the current state of competition (being the likely future state of competition if the Acquisition does not proceed).
Relevant areas of competition

The ACCC considers that the relevant areas of competitive overlap for the purposes of its assessment of the competitive effects of the Acquisition are the supply of residential and commercial mortgage broking services in Australia.

The ACCC considers it likely that there are separate markets for residential, and commercial mortgage broking. Individual home buyers for residential mortgage broking and commercial borrowers (corporate clients, property developers, commercial real estate investors etc.) are likely to have different needs. Commercial lending arrangements are also likely to be more complex than residential home loans and require specialist mortgage broking skill sets.
Competition effects in the supply of residential and commercial mortgage broking services

The ACCC considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition in relation to the supply of residential and/or commercial mortgage broking services in Australia for the reasons set out below:

items: REA Group’s Mortgage Choice and Simplicity are unlikely to be close substitutes as they serve different customers with different needs. Based on information from the merger parties, Mortgage Choice’s core customer base comprises of individual home buyers for residential mortgage broking with a small number of commercial settlements while Simplicity’s core business is in commercial mortgages with minimal residential mortgages;

Based on information provided by the merger parties and publicly available information, the market share aggregation arising from the Acquisition is estimated to be low on any market definition, with an estimated post-acquisition market share of below 5 per cent;

Based on information from the merger parties and publicly available information, the merged firm would likely continue to be constrained by other residential mortgage brokers such as Aussie Home Loans, LMG and Lendi; and commercial mortgage brokers such as Stamford Capital Australia, Green Finance Group and Rethink Financing.

determination_statement_of_reasons.text: Non-horizontal effects in commercial mortgage broking services

The ACCC considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition because the merged entity is unlikely to have the ability and/or incentive to harm the competitiveness of rival commercial mortgage brokers, for example by limiting referrals of commercial borrowing leads from Mortgage Choice or its online real estate advertising platforms to Simplicity for the reasons set out below:

items: Based on information from the merger parties and market feedback, Mortgage Choice is not a significant source of leads for commercial borrowers and post- acquisition, individual Mortgage Choice brokers will continue to retain discretion over the commercial brokers to which they choose to refer commercial leads;

Commercial brokers are not reliant on referrals from residential brokers. Market feedback suggests the most important referral channels for commercial brokers are existing client relationships, professional referral networks, real estate agents and direct marketing;

While REA Group will generate some leads from its online real estate advertising platforms, these leads are unlikely to have a significant impact on the ability of alternative mortgage brokers to remain competitive;

Post-acquisition, the merged firm would continue to face competition from alternative suppliers of commercial borrower referral programs or platforms, which enables advisors and residential mortgage brokers to refer commercial borrower leads.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Coles Group Limited (**Coles**) is an Australian public company (ASX:COL) that primarily operates supermarkets and liquor stores. Coles also has a property development arm, Coles Group Property Developments Ltd (**CGPD**), that buys land and constructs shopping centres, supermarkets and liquor outlets to then sell to third party buyers. Powercor Australia Ltd (**Powercor**) is the electricity distribution company responsible for electricity distribution in the Yarrawonga area.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by a division of the Commission constituted by a direction issued pursuant to section 19 of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Coles Group Property Developments Ltd (CGPD) applied for a
notification waiver in respect of its proposed acquisition of a parcel
of land at (Res 2) Frank Drive, Yarrawonga VIC (target land) from
Powercor Australia Ltd (Powercor), as described in the transaction
documents provided as part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, CGPD, is a property development arm of Coles Group
Limited (Coles). Coles is an Australian public company (ASX:COL)
that primarily operates supermarkets and liquor stores. CGPD buys
land and constructs shopping centres, supermarkets and liquor
outlets to then sell to third party buyers.
The vendor, Powercor, is the electricity distribution company
responsible for electricity distribution in the Yarrawonga area.
The target land is located in Yarrawonga, VIC and is currently used
as an electricity substation. The target land is adjacent to a new
Coles supermarket and Liquorland store being opened at Kaiela
Business Park, 14 Frank Drive, Yarrawonga VIC 3730 (Coles
Yarrawonga).
The target land would be used to provide Coles Yarrawonga with a
permanent electricity supply.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
a. the target land is not currently used for grocery retailing and
will be used to supply permanent electricity to Coles
Yarrawonga
b. Coles does not have an existing supermarket or liquor store
in Yarrawonga
c. there are alternative grocery retailers, including Woolworths,
Aldi and IGA, and retail suppliers of packaged liquor for
offsite consumption in the local area.
Given that material competition concerns are unlikely to arise, the
ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
In these circumstances, the ACCC does not consider it necessary to
reach a concluded view on the likelihood of the notification
thresholds determined under section 51ABP(1) being met. The ACCC
does however observe that the targeted notification requirements for
supermarkets determined under section 51ABQ(1) are likely to apply.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

determination_table_content.details:
Lumina Japan Acquisition Co., Ltd (Lumina Japan), a special
purpose vehicle of funds managed or advised by affiliates of Apollo
Capital Management, L.P., applied for a notification waiver in respect
of its proposed indirect acquisition of sole control of Nippon Sheet
Glass Company, Limited (Nippon Sheet Glass), as described in the
transaction documents provided as part of the application (the
Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, Lumina Japan, is a special purpose vehicle of funds
managed or advised by affiliates of Apollo Capital Management, L.P.,
which is a wholly owned indirect subsidiary of Apollo Global
Management, Inc. (together with its indirect subsidiaries, Apollo). For
the purposes of this document, Apollo and the investment funds
managed by Apollo’s affiliates are referred to as the Apollo Funds.
Apollo is a high-growth global alternative asset manager
headquartered in New York City and incorporated in the US. It offers
investment management and financial planning services to
individuals and institutional investors on a global basis. Examples of
Apollo’s current investments include, inter alia, companies in
education, insurance, financial services, and leisure businesses.
The target, Nippon Sheet Glass, is a global manufacturer and
supplier of a wide range of glass products, operating across three
global business units: architectural glass, automotive glass, and
creative technology glass (or technical glass).

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver and had regard
to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular, there is no horizontal overlap
or vertical relationship between the Apollo Funds’ controlled portfolio
companies and Nippon Sheet Glass in Australia.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

events.title: Eli Lilly - acquisition of licence from CSL Behring - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Eli Lilly and Company (Eli Lilly) and CSL Behring LLC (CSL Behring)
propose to enter into a licence agreement under which Eli Lilly would
be granted certain exclusive rights to develop and commercialise
Clazakizumab, an anti-interleukin-6 (IL-6) monoclonal antibody, for
certain new indications including atrial fibrillation, atherosclerotic
cardiovascular disease and other cardiovascular indications (the
Acquisition). Under the licence agreement, CSL Behring would retain
exclusive rights to continue the development and commercialisation
of Clazakizumab for the prevention of cardiovascular events in
patients with end stage kidney disease.

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Eli Lilly, is a US-based pharmaceutical company which
discovers, develops, manufactures and markets human
pharmaceutical products for various therapeutic areas, including
diabetes, oncology, immunology and neuroscience. Eli Lilly is
publicly listed on the New York Stock Exchange.
The target, CSL Behring, is a subsidiary of CSL Limited, a publicly
listed global biopharmaceutical business, which is headquartered in
Australia and listed on the Australian Stock Exchange (ASX:CSL).
CSL Limited develops, manufactures and markets therapies derived
from human plasma and recombinant technologies for rare and
serious diseases. It provides treatments for immunology,
hematology, cardiovascular and metabolic disorders.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
Both parties are active in the development, manufacturing and
marketing of human pharmaceutical products in Australia.
Clazakizumab is currently not commercialised by CSL Limited, and
Eli Lilly does not market any IL-6 inhibitors or have any such
products in clinical development. Eli Lilly does not market any other
treatments for atrial fibrillation or atherosclerotic cardiovascular
disease.
Eli Lilly is developing other therapies for the treatment of
atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, one of the indications for
which it plans to develop Clazakizumab. However, there are
significant differences between these other pipeline therapies and
Clazakizumab, including that the other therapies are not anti-
inflammatory and that they have different therapeutic approaches,
mechanisms of action, modality and target patients.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form and information from third parties.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• It appears unlikely that Eli Lilly’s existing pipeline therapies under
development for atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (if they
can be commercialised) would compete with Clazakizumab (if it
can also be commercialised) because of fundamental
differences between the therapies.
• There is no competitive overlap between Eli Lilly’s and CSL
Behring’s research, development or marketing activities relating
to IL-6 inhibitors.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

determination_table_content.details:
Sembcorp Industries Ltd (Sembcorp) together with Sembcorp’s
indirect wholly-owned subsidiaries, Sembcorp Australia Pty Ltd and
Sembcorp Energy Australia Pte Ltd (collectively, the Purchasers),
propose to acquire by way of share sale agreement, 100% of the
issued share capital in Pioneer Sail Holdings Pty Ltd (Pioneer Sail
Holdings) and its subsidiaries (including Alinta Energy Pty Ltd
(Alinta Energy) and Latrobe Valley Power (Holdings) Pty Ltd
(together, the Target Group)) from Chow Tai Fook Enterprises
Limited and Pioneer Sail Singapore Pte Ltd (collectively, the Sellers)
(the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Sembcorp, is an energy, renewable and industrial
solutions company headquartered in Singapore with renewable
energy and gas assets in the wider Asia region. Sembcorp does not
own any operating assets in Australia, nor have any minority or
controlling interests in any entities active in the energy sector in
Australia.
Alinta Energy is an Australian "gentailer" which supplies electricity
and gas to customers across Australia. It has generation assets
comprising of coal, gas, onshore wind and solar and a development
pipeline of renewable energy projects. Latrobe Valley Power
(Holdings) Pty Ltd operates the Loy Yang B Power Station, a coal-
fired power station in Victoria.
Temasek Holdings (Private) Ltd (Temasek), the largest shareholder
of Sembcorp, has an indirect interest in SGSP (Australia) Assets Pty
Ltd (SGSPAA), the owner of Jemena. Jemena is the owner and
operator of gas distribution and transmission and electricity
distribution network assets on the east coast of Australia and has
interests in three electricity distribution networks located in New
South Wales, Victoria and the Australian Capital Territory.
Temasek also has an indirect interest in Neoen SA (Neoen), a
France-headquartered global developer, constructor, financier and
operator of renewable energy assets. Neoen owns and/or operates a
number of wind, solar and energy storage projects in New South
Wales, Queensland, South Australia, Tasmania, and Western
Australia.

The reasons for the ACCC’s determination are set out in section 2.

determination_statement_of_reasons.text: When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines and interim merger process guidelines.

In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered the information and documents that were submitted with the notification form, additional information provided by the merger parties, market feedback and publicly available information.
For the reasons given below, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into effect.
Industry background
National Electricity Market

Electricity generated in eastern and southern Australia is traded through the National Electricity Market (NEM).1 The NEM consists of a wholesale spot market for selling electricity and a transmission grid for transporting it to energy customers.2 The market covers 5 regions – Queensland, New South Wales including the Australian Capital Territory, Victoria, South Australia and Tasmania, and services around 11 million customers.3

The NEM is operated by the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) through a centrally-coordinated dispatch process that pools generation from producers and delivers required quantities of electricity from the pool to wholesale consumers. This includes managing the bidding, scheduling and dispatch of generators, determining the spot price, measuring electricity use, and financially settling the market.4
Wholesale Electricity Market

Like the NEM, the Wholesale Electricity Market (WEM) is an electricity market that enables wholesale electricity sales between generators and retailers, large-scale customers and demand side participants. The market supplies energy to south-west Western Australia and operates on the South West Interconnected System (SWIS).5

The WEM has an additional capacity market, the Reserve Capacity Mechanism, which is unique to the WEM and aims to ensure there is sufficient generation capacity in the SWIS. 6 The Reserve Capacity Mechanism incentivises investment by awarding tradeable capacity credits to suppliers to guarantee capacity for the SWIS in a given year. 1 AER, State of the Energy Market 2025, 26 August 2025, p. 14. 2 AER, State of the Energy Market 2025, 26 August 2025, p. 16. 3 AER, State of the Energy Market 2025, 26 August 2025, p. 14. 4 AEMO, Dispatch information, accessed 30 April 2026. 5 AEMO, Energy markets and systems, accessed 30 April 2026. 6 AEMO, Energy markets and systems, accessed 30 April 2026.
Regulation of gas and electricity networks

Australia’s electricity and gas network assets are regulated under the National Electricity Law (NEL) and the National Electricity Rules (NER), and the National Gas Law (NGL) and National Gas Rules (NGR), respectively.
The ACCC considers the access and transparency and ring-fencing and non-disclosure obligations of these regulatory frameworks to be most relevant to its assessment of the Acquisition.
Transparency and access obligations
The NER and NGR set out a number of transparency and access obligations which apply to Jemena’s electricity distribution networks and gas transmission and distribution pipelines, respectively.

Chapter 3 of the NER includes requirements for the provision of information by, and to, AEMO, including information about existing and proposed generation projects, network congestion, available capacity of generators and networks, adequacy of electricity supply, the operation of the spot market, and requirements for ancillary services.7 In addition, information regarding proposed and approved revenue and pricing by Distribution Network Service Providers (DNSPs) is made available through the Australian Energy Regulator’s (AER) public draft and final determinations pursuant to Chapter 6 of the NER.8

Chapter 5 of the NER provides frameworks for establishing and modifying connection and access to electricity networks by generators, including through published standard offers for connection and processes for negotiated connections and dispute resolution.9

Part 10 of the NGR prescribes information that gas pipeline service providers must make publicly available on their websites, which includes demand, capacity and pricing information.10 Access to this information is designed to assist users in negotiations with pipeline service providers.

Gas pipelines owned by Jemena are subject to a negotiate-arbitrate model under the NGL and NGR. This framework is designed to promote negotiated access to gas pipeline services, with the ability for access disputes to be arbitrated. Access disputes can include price and non-price matters.11
Ring-fencing and non-disclosure obligations

Ring-fencing refers to separating the regulated activities of an electricity DNSP or gas pipeline operator from contestable parts of their business. These requirements include restrictions on sharing information obtained through regulated electricity or gas 7 Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC), Chapter 3 of the NER. 8 AEMC, Chapter 6 of the NER. 9 AEMC, Chapter 5 of the NER. 10 AEMC, Part 10 of the NGR. 11 AER, Pipeline Access Dispute Guide, November 2023. operations with related businesses that operate in contestable markets, for example in retailing electricity or gas.

The AER’s Electricity Distribution Ring-fencing Guideline, made under the NER, imposes obligations designed to prevent cross-subsidisation and discriminatory conduct between an electricity DNSP’s regulated network services and its activities in contestable markets. 12

Ring-fencing obligations apply to non-public information which a DNSP acquires or generates in connection with its regulated operations, including information concerning individual customers or classes of customers. A DNSP is generally:

items: required to protect the confidentiality of that information, and only use the information for the purpose for which it was acquired or generated. This obligation is subject to certain exceptions including consent, complying with legal requirements or where required for the DNSP or requesting party to provide services
prohibited from disclosing information to a related business where it is obtained from a current or potential competitor and would likely advantage the related business, and
required to provide access to such information to competitors of a related business on request where it has disclosed such information to a related business.13

determination_statement_of_reasons.text: Rule 8.6 of the NER sets out obligations for registered participants in the NEM (including DNSPs) regarding confidential or commercially sensitive information. These provisions generally require participants to use all reasonable endeavours to keep confidential any confidential information that comes into its possession or control, or of which it becomes aware. It contains several exceptions, including consent, complying with legal requirements and disclosure in connection with arbitration or legal proceedings.14

Gas distribution networks are also subject to ring-fencing obligations under the NGL. These ring-fencing provisions require the separation of pipeline businesses from related businesses, such as those that produce, buy or sell gas.

Additionally, Part 16 of the NGR sets out obligations relating to the protection of confidential information against improper use or disclosure, subject to limited exceptions including consent, complying with legal requirements and disclosure in connection with arbitration or legal proceedings. These obligations require gas pipeline operators to take all practical steps to protect confidential information from improper disclosure or use and to, upon request, disclose specific information to the relevant person to whom the information relates (or a person acting on their behalf).15 12 AER, Ring-fencing Guideline Electricity Distribution Version 4, 27 February 2025. 13 AER, Ring-fencing Guideline Electricity Distribution Version 4, 27 February 2025, clauses 4.1 and 4.3. 14 AEMC, Rule 8.6 of the NER. 15 AEMC, Part 16 of the NGR.
Overlap and relationship between the parties

Sembcorp itself does not own any operating assets in Australia or have any minority or controlling interests in any entities active in the energy sector in Australia. However, Temasek, the largest shareholder of Sembcorp, has indirect minority interests in both Jemena and Neoen, which give rise to vertical links and horizontal overlaps respectively. Temasek and its related entities are referred to in these reasons as
Temasek Group.
Relationships between Jemena and the Target Group

Temasek is an indirect, minority shareholder of Jemena through its wholly owned subsidiary, Singapore Power Limited (SP Group). Singapore Power International Pte Ltd (SPI), a subsidiary of SP Group, has a 40% interest in Jemena’s parent company, SGSPAA, with the remaining equity held by State Grid International Development Australia Investment Company Ltd (State Grid) as SGSPAA’s controlling shareholder.

Jemena is an owner and operator of gas transmission and distribution and electricity distribution network assets on the east coast of Australia. Jemena owns or otherwise has interests in three electricity distribution networks, located in the ACT and Victoria, and two gas distribution networks, located in the ACT and NSW. Jemena also owns gas transmission pipelines on the east coast of Australia (listed in Table 1 below).

Jemena’s gas and electricity assets which have a relationship to Alinta Energy’s operations are detailed in Table 1 below. There are no other vertical relationships between Jemena and other entities in the Target Group. Table 1 Jemena assets and relationship with Alinta Energy Asset type State Relationship with Alinta Energy Gas transmission Eastern Gas Pipeline NSW/VIC Alinta Energy’s Bairnsdale Power Station connects to the Eastern Gas Pipeline. There are no other gas-fired power stations connected to the Eastern Gas Pipeline in Victoria. A number of competing gas-fired power stations also connect to the Eastern Gas Pipeline in NSW including Energy Australia's 760MW Tallawarra power station. VicHub Interconnect Facility VIC Alinta Energy utilises the VicHub Interconnect to transport gas from the Victorian Declared Wholesale Gas Market to customers along the Eastern Gas Pipeline or in the Sydney Short Term Trading Market. Gas distribution Jemena Gas Distribution NSW Alinta Energy utilises Jemena's gas distribution Network network to deliver gas to its retail customers in NSW. Information from the parties indicates that Alinta Energy has a less than 10% share of the retail supply of gas customers to each of C&I and small-use customers in NSW. Electricity distribution Jemena Electricity Distribution VIC Alinta Energy utilises the Jemena and United Network Energy electricity distribution networks to deliver electricity to its retail customers in Victoria. VIC Information from the parties indicates that Alinta United Energy Distribution Energy has a less than 10% share of the retail Network (34% share) supply of gas customers to each of C&I and small-use customers in Victoria.
As outlined in Table 1 above, Jemena’s infrastructure assets overlap vertically with Alinta Energy’s:

items: electricity generation operations in Victoria
retail supply of gas in New South Wales, and
retail supply of electricity in Victoria.

determination_statement_of_reasons.text: Horizontal overlap with Neoen

Temasek has an indirect 14.19% interest in Neoen. Neoen is a France-headquartered global developer, constructor, financier and operator of renewable energy assets. Within Australia, Neoen owns and/or operates a number of wind, solar and energy storage projects in New South Wales, Queensland, South Australia, Tasmania and Western Australia.

Alinta Energy and Neoen overlap in the generation and supply of wholesale electricity to each of the NEM and WEM, as shown in Table 2. There are no other overlaps between Neoen and other entities in the Target Group. Table 2 Horizontal overlaps between Neoen and Alinta Energy State Alinta Energy asset Neoen generation asset Queensland Braemar Power Station (564 MW) Western Downs Green Power Hub (460 MW) Collinsville Solar Farm (PPA) (29 MW) Western Downs Battery Stage 1 (270 MW) Rugby Run Solar Farm (PPA) (52 MW) Western Downs Battery Stage 2 (270 MW) Kaban Green Power Hub (157 MW) New South Wellington North Solar Farm (PPA) (32 Dubbo Solar Farm (28 MW) Wales MW) Parkes Solar Farm (66 MW) Griffith Solar Farm (36 MW) Western Pinjarra Cogeneration Plant (285 MW) Collie Battery Stage 1 (219 MW) Australia Wagerup Power Station (412 MW) Collie Battery Stage 2 (341 MW) Yandin Wind Farm (214.2 MW) Badgingarra Wind and Solar Farm (PPA) (150 MW) Walkaway Wind Farm (PPA) (89 MW)

There is no current overlap between Alinta Energy and Neoen in South Australia. However, Alinta Energy does have some projects in development, including the Reeves Plain Energy Hub (300 MW), and Neoen has existing facilities including the Goyder South Wind Farm (412 MW) and Blyth Battery (238.5 MW).

Neoen and Alinta Energy also both supply storage and firming capacity to the WEM. Alinta Energy does not currently have any storage and firming projects in the NEM but has several projects in development. Similarly, both parties participate in markets for the supply of essential system services and frequency control services and electricity derivatives and contracting markets.
Competition assessment

The ACCC has considered the effects of the Acquisition by comparing the likely future state of competition if the Acquisition proceeds against the continuation of the current state of competition (being the likely future state of competition if the Acquisition does not proceed).

Operators of monopoly electricity network assets and gas pipelines tend to have substantial market power. Regulation and market design, including in the operation of the NEM and WEM, seeks to constrain the exercise of that market power. Despite this, vertical integration involving monopoly energy infrastructure assets has the potential to raise material competition concerns that require close consideration. Operators of monopoly energy infrastructure assets may leverage their market power into vertically related markets to foreclose competition through discrimination against rivals, preferential treatment of affiliates or sharing competitively significant information.
Vertical effects – foreclosure of rival electricity generators in Victoria and the NEM

The ACCC considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition in relation to the generation and wholesale supply of electricity in Victoria and the NEM. The ACCC considered the supply of storage and firming capacity, essential system support services and frequency control services, and derivatives within the broader assessment of wholesale electricity rivalry in the circumstances of this matter.

The ACCC has considered the ability and incentive of the merged entity to hinder, delay or otherwise frustrate rival electricity generators in a way that would substantially lessen competition, through its ownership links with Jemena. Cross-ownership between Alinta Energy’s market energy operations and Jemena’s monopoly infrastructure electricity network assets and gas transmission pipeline assets, has the potential to raise vertical foreclosure risks that need to be closely considered.

The ACCC has had regard to the level of control and influence that Temasek Group has over Jemena in order to assess the likely vertical effects of the Acquisition. Based on information and documents provided by the merger parties and third parties, the ACCC accepts that Temasek Group is unlikely to be able to positively direct Jemena’s decision making or operations. However, the ACCC considers that Temasek Group is able to influence certain investment decisions by SGSPAA.

In coming to this view, the ACCC has had regard to the size of Temasek Group’s shareholding in SGSPAA, the rights, interests and incentives of State Grid, the composition of the SGSPAA board, how critical operational decisions are made by Jemena, and the governance arrangements and conflicts policies that exist within Jemena.

The ACCC’s assessment of vertical effects in electricity generation has focused on the potential for Temasek Group to foreclose the Target Group’s electricity generation rivals by hindering or preventing Jemena’s investment in its gas/electricity network and gas transmission assets as required to facilitate access or expansion by generation rivals.
Based on information from the merger parties, market feedback and publicly available information, the ACCC considers that:

items: There are no committed or anticipated material generation projects which require further investment in Jemena’s gas or electricity assets that the ACCC is currently aware of.

Rival electricity generators may be able to access sufficient alternatives to Jemena’s gas and electricity assets, which would limit the ability and incentive to foreclose. For example, gas-fired generation rivals may be able to access inputs through alternative gas pipelines or receipt points, and most generators connect to electricity transmission networks (where Jemena does not have any interests) rather than electricity distribution networks.

A strategy to foreclose a material rival generator may not be profitable for Temasek Group. Post-acquisition, Temasek Group will hold similar partial interests in Jemena and the Target Group (via Sembcorp). A foreclosure strategy could result in a loss of profits for Temasek Group through Jemena not supplying services, or supplying a lower volume of services, to the rival generator (unless the Target Group connects a generator to Jemena’s network at the expense of the foreclosed rival). The Target Group would likely only recapture a very small proportion of those lost profits downstream given: − its less than 10% share of registered capacity in Victoria as well as across the NEM, and − stymieing a specific rival generator is likely to create market conditions that may support a different generator entering the market, which is not reliant on connecting to Jemena’s network assets or gas supply from Jemena’s gas transmission assets.

The regulatory frameworks in electricity and gas markets (described at paragraphs 2.9–2.21 above), may mitigate the likelihood of Temasek Group utilising its minority shareholding in Jemena to prevent or hinder network investment.

determination_statement_of_reasons.text: Having regard to the combination of factors identified in paragraph 2.38 above, the ACCC considers that Temasek Group would be unlikely to have the ability and/or incentive post-Acquisition to foreclose rival electricity generators through its ownership links with Jemena.
Vertical effects – foreclosure of Alinta Energy’s rivals in the retail supply of gas in New South Wales and retail supply of electricity in Victoria

In the ACCC’s view, the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition in relation to the retail supply of gas in New South Wales and the retail supply of electricity in Victoria because Alinta Energy would be unlikely to have the ability and/or incentive post-Acquisition to harm the competitiveness of rivals in a way that substantially lessens competition through its access to and use of competitively significant information.

In this case, the ACCC focused on the potential for Alinta Energy, as part of the merged entity, to access and use competitively significant information held by Jemena to undermine the ability and incentive of rival gas and electricity retailers to compete effectively. For example, information about rival retailers’ customers or innovative business customer/household energy solutions that require the involvement of the network business.

The ACCC considers that information would generally need to be quite specific, down to the level of individual or small groups of customers, in order for Alinta Energy to gain competitive advantages. Higher-level information about market shares and strategies is unlikely to be competitively significant, as such information is commonly available in the market.

Alinta Energy’s access to competitively significant information held by Jemena would likely be contrary to Jemena’s regulatory obligations under Part 16 of the NGR, Rule 8.6 of the NER and AER Ring Fencing Guidelines (see paragraphs 2.17-2.21 above).

These regulatory requirements may, to some extent, mitigate Alinta Energy’s ability to access and use competitively significant information held by Jemena. However, the ACCC also considers that there is a degree of circumvention risk where a party has an incentive to engage in conduct which is contrary to regulatory requirements.
Nonetheless, the ACCC considers that foreclosure strategies by Alinta Energy which utilise competitively significant information are unlikely because:

items: Temasek Group is unlikely to have sufficient positive control over Jemena (as identified at paragraph 2.35 above) to combine Alinta Energy’s access to competitively significant information with preferential network treatment from Jemena

any use of competitively significant information in the absence of preferential network treatment would likely only provide transient advantages to Alinta Energy in retail supply as competitors respond to Alinta Energy’s observed commercial strategies and/or become aware of the information because of transparency and access requirements that apply to Jemena (described at paragraphs 2.11-2.15 above), and
transient benefits from the use of competitively significant information may not offset the regulatory risks involved with the disclosure and use of that information.

determination_statement_of_reasons.text: Reduced competition arising from horizontal aggregation in the generation and supply of wholesale electricity to each of the NEM and the WEM

The ACCC considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to substantially lessen competition in the generation and supply of wholesale electricity to the NEM and/or WEM as a result of the removal of competition between Alinta Energy and Neoen. The ACCC considered the supply of storage and firming capacity, essential system support services and frequency control services, and derivatives within the broader assessment of wholesale electricity rivalry in the circumstances of this matter.
The ACCC has had regard to the level of control and influence that Temasek Group has over Neoen as part of its assessment of the potential for reduced competition post- Acquisition.

In the ACCC’s view, Temasek Group is unlikely to be able to exercise positive control or material influence over Neoen’s operations. In coming to this view, the ACCC has had regard to information provided by the merger parties about the size of Temasek Group’s indirect shareholding (at 14.19%) relative to other shareholders, arrangements for the appointment of directors on the Neoen board, how critical operational decisions are made by Neoen, and governance arrangements within Neoen.
Although Temasek Group will hold interests in both Alinta Energy and Neoen, based on the material before it, the ACCC considers that:

items: Temasek’s minor interest in Neoen makes it unlikely that Alinta Energy and Neoen will compete as a single entity in the generation and supply of wholesale electricity, and their incentives to compete are unlikely to materially change as a result of the Acquisition.

Even if Alinta Energy and Neoen competed as a single entity, it would have a relatively small market share. Based on information from the merger parties: − Alinta Energy and Neoen would have a combined share of 10% of registered capacity in the Queensland region of the NEM and less than 5% in the New South Wales region of the NEM. They do not overlap in other NEM states. Across the NEM, Alinta Energy and Neoen would have a combined, <10% share of total registered capacity or dispatchable electricity capacity in the NEM. − Although the combined Alinta Energy/Neoen would have a higher share of registered capacity in the WEM (20-30%), as highlighted in market feedback, the ACCC notes that these figures may overstate the significance of the overlap as they do not reflect differences in generation portfolios or in load factor and intermittency.

Similarly, Alinta Energy and Neoen will continue to face competition from alternative suppliers of wholesale electricity. Competitors in the NEM include AGL Energy, Origin Energy, Stanwell and Energy Australia. In the WEM, alternative generators include Synergy and NewGen and BlueWaters (which are both owned by Sumitomo Corporation).
