determination_table_content.details:
Ryowa II GPS Pty Ltd (Ryowa), whose ultimate parent company is
Mayfair Australia Corporation Pty Ltd (Mayfair Corporation), applied
for a notification waiver in respect of its proposed acquisition of:
a. all or substantially all of the assets of the Phosphate Hill
fertiliser mine and manufacturing plant (Phosphate Hill), and
b. 100% of the issued share capital in PH Queensland Gas Pty
Ltd (PHQG)
from Dyno Nobel Limited (DNL) and Southern Cross Operations Pty
Ltd (SCO), as described in the transaction documents provided as
part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, Ryowa, currently owns a minority interest in the
Gladstone Power Station. Ryowa’s ultimate parent company, Mayfair
Corporation, is an Australia energy and resources company.
The target, Phosphate Hill, is currently owned by DNL, an
international explosive manufacturing business. In Australia, DNL
manufactures and supplies commercial explosives and provides
commercial blasting services. SCO is a wholly owned subsidiary of
DNL, which currently operates in the fertiliser manufacturing
business through Phosphate Hill.
PHQG owns a minority interest in the Range Gas Project, a
development project in Queensland’s Surat Basin.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
certain publicly available information, and had regard to the factors
in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010
(Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular, there is no horizontal overlap or vertical
relationship between the merger parties in Australia.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

determination_table_content.details:
Apollo Purchaser, Inc. (Apollo Purchaser) applied for a notification
waiver in respect of its proposed acquisition of all of the issued and
outstanding shares in Blackline Safety Corp. (together with its
subsidiaries, Blackline Safety), as described in the transaction
documents provided as part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, Apollo Purchaser, is a special purpose vehicle which is
ultimately controlled by vehicles comprising funds advised by
Francisco Partners Management L.P. (together with its affiliated
funds, Francisco Partners). Francisco Partners is a global
investment firm specialising in technology and technology-enabled
businesses, headquartered in San Francisco, United States with
offices in New York and London.
The target, Blackline Safety, is a public company registered in
Canada, headquartered in Calgary and listed on the Toronto Stock
Exchange (TSE: BLN). Blackline Safety specialises in designing and
manufacturing hardware enabled Software-as-a-Service products for
worker safety monitoring. Its products include wearable devices,
personal and area gas monitors and cloud-based data analytics
software. Blackline Safety’s products allow employers to monitor
and communicate with lone workers and detect possible leakage of
gas or other hazardous materials that may pose a threat to workers.
The accompanying software streams data from devices in the field
and enables employers to manage emergencies.
Blackline Safety has a worldwide presence with customers in more
than 75 countries, including Australia. Blackline Safety has an
Australian subsidiary, Blackline Safety Australia Pty Ltd.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. there is no competitive overlap between Francisco Partners
(including its portfolio companies with operations in
Australia) and Blackline Safety in the manufacturing and
supply of worker safety monitoring equipment (such as gas
detection devices and lone worker SOS devices) in Australia
b. there are alternative manufacturers and suppliers of worker
safety monitoring equipment in Australia, including global
and domestic suppliers.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

events.title: Ventura Motors / Crown Coaches - Phase 1 Determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Philip Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Ventura Motors NewCo Pty Ltd, a wholly owned subsidiary of
Ventura Motors Pty Ltd (together with its subsidiaries, Ventura
Motors) proposes to acquire the business assets (including the
vehicles, plant and equipment, IP and IP licences, goodwill, and
business records) of Crown Coaches Pty Ltd and Crown Hybrid Pty
Ltd that constitute the Crown Coaches business (Crown Coaches)
(the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Ventura Motors, is an Australian bus operator primarily
active in metropolitan Melbourne. It supplies metropolitan bus
services, public school bus services, and private school and other
private charter bus services, as well as regional intercity coach
transport.
The target, Crown Coaches, is a family-owned bus operator that
supplies public school bus services, and private school and other
private charter bus services in metropolitan Melbourne.

Ventura Motors and Crown Coaches both supply:
• bus services to public specialised schools in metropolitan
Melbourne
• private charter bus services in metropolitan Melbourne.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, information from third partes, and publicly
available information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• Notwithstanding a concern expressed by a third party that the
Acquisition may reduce options available to customers, post-
acquisition, the merged firm would likely continue to face
competition from alternative suppliers of public specialised
school bus services and private charter bus services in
metropolitan Melbourne.
• The aggregation arising from the Acquisition is low in relation to
public specialised school bus services and private charter bus
services in metropolitan Melbourne.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

deals_cleared.merger_name: BHP – expansion of Special Mining Lease at Olympic Dam, SA

acquirers.name: BHP OLYMPIC DAM CORPORATION PTY LTD

deals_cleared.merger_description: The parties to this acquisition are BHP Olympic Dam Corporation Pty Ltd, which is ultimately 100% owned by BHP Group Limited (**BHP**), and the State of South Australia.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Philip Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
BHP Olympic Dam Corporation Pty Ltd, a subsidiary of BHP Group
Limited (BHP), applied for a notification waiver in respect of its
proposed acquisition of mining rights over an additional area of land
located in Olympic Dam, South Australia from the State of South
Australia, as described in the transaction documents provided as
part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

BHP is an Australian headquartered natural resources company.
BHP is a global leading producer of iron ore, copper and
metallurgical coal. Relevantly, BHP operates the Olympic Dam Mine
in South Australia, which is one of most significant global mineral
deposits.
The State of South Australia through the Department of Energy and
Mining, is responsible for regulating mining rights in South Australia
and owns/manages the underlying legal rights to the minerals.
BHP proposes to acquire mining rights over an additional area of
land located at Olympic Dam, as well as additional non-proprietary
rights in relation to its existing operations on the Olympic Dam mine.
The additional area is adjacent to BHP’s existing special mining
lease at Olympic Dam.
The main mineral mined at Olympic Dam is copper, with gold, silver
and uranium as by-products.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
a. there is no removal of a competitor as a result of the
Acquisition
b. the Acquisition is unlikely to affect BHP’s ability to influence
the domestic price of copper, as prices are typically set by
reference to global conditions
c. there are alternative producers and suppliers of copper, gold,
silver and uranium in Australia and globally.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Daikin Industries, Ltd (**Daikin**), through its wholly owned subsidiary, Airmaster Corporation Pty Limited (**Airmaster**), proposes to acquire Australian Air Conditioning & Mechanical Services Group (**AAMS Group**) via an asset acquisition from Damday Pty Ltd. The assets of the AAMS Group business being acquired includes all plant and equipment, goodwill, stock, customer and supplier contracts, and AAMS Group’s leased premises (the **Acquisition**).

events.title: Daikin - AAMS Group - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Philip Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Daikin Industries, Ltd (Daikin)’s, through its wholly owned subsidiary,
Airmaster Corporation Pty Limited (Airmaster), proposed acquisition
of Australian Air Conditioning & Mechanical Services Group (AAMS
Group) via an asset acquisition from Damday Pty Ltd (the
Acquisition). The assets of the AAMS Group business being
acquired includes all plant and equipment, goodwill, stock, customer
and supplier contracts and AAMS Group’s leased premises.

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Daikin, operates in Australia through its wholly owned
subsidiary, Daikin Australia Pty Ltd (Daikin Australia). Daikin
Australia’s principal activities consist of the manufacture and
distribution of air conditioning equipment.
Daikin Australia wholly owns Airmaster Corporation Pty Limited
(Airmaster). Airmaster is a building services company which
supplies Heating, Ventilation, Air Conditioning (HVAC), refrigeration,
Building Management Systems (BMS), fire maintenance, servicing
and installations to Commercial and Industrial (C&I) customers.
Airmaster has a presence in each state and territory except the
Northern Territory.
The target, AAMS Group, is a mechanical services contractor
operating in construction, HVAC and mechanical services industries.
It designs, fabricates, supplies and installs HVAC systems and
provides ongoing servicing and maintenance services to C&I
customers. AAMS Group operates in Darwin.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, information from third parties and publicly
available information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• There is minimal geographic overlap between Daikin and AAMS
Group in the provision of HVAC and related services.
• Following the Acquisition, Daikin will continue to be constrained
by rival national and Northern Territory based HVAC and related
services providers, including VAE Group, Total Ventilation
Hygiene Services, Airducter and Service Air.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

deals_declined.merger_description: Herron Todd White (Bidco) Pty Ltd (**HTW Bidco**) proposes to acquire 100% of the shares in CJA Lee Property Pty Ltd (**CJA Lee Property**) from C McMahon Enterprises Pty Ltd as trustee for the C&N McMahon Family Trust (**C McMahon Enterprises**) (50%) and T.J. Shaw Pty Ltd as trustee for the T.J. Shaw Family Trust (**T.J. Shaw**) (50%) (the **Acquisition**). As part of the Acquisition, C McMahon Enterprises and T.J. Shaw will be issued shares in the ultimate parent company of HTW Bidco – Herron Todd White (Holdings) Pty Ltd (**HTW Holdings**). Post-completion, C McMahon Enterprises and T.J. Shaw will own 0.15% of the shares in HTW Holdings.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by a division of the Commission constituted by a direction issued pursuant to section 19 of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Herron Todd White (Bidco) Pty Ltd (HTW Bidco) applied for a
notification waiver in respect of its proposed acquisition of 100% of
the shares in CJA Lee Property Pty Ltd (CJA Lee Property) from C
McMahon Enterprises Pty Ltd as trustee for the C&N McMahon
Family Trust (C McMahon Enterprises) and T.J. Shaw Pty Ltd as
trustee for the T.J. Shaw Family Trust (T.J. Shaw), as described in
the transaction documents provided as part of the application (the
Acquisition).
As part of the Acquisition, C McMahon Enterprises and T.J. Shaw
will be issued shares in Herron Todd White (Holdings) Pty Ltd. Post-
completion, they will own 0.15% of the shares in that entity.

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(b) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) not to make the notification waiver
determination applied for.

The acquirer, HTW Bidco, is a subsidiary of Herron Todd White
(Holdings) Pty Ltd (together with its fully owned subsidiaries, HTW
Group). The HTW Group provides property valuation and property
advisory services in Australia. HTW Group primarily provides
residential property valuation services to Australian authorised
deposit-taking institutions.
HTW Group is majority-owned by funds managed by QPE No. 7
Management Pty Ltd, an entity within the Quadrant Private Equity
group (QPE). Apart from HTW Group, QPE is not involved in the
supply of property valuation and property advisory services in
Australia.
CJA Lee Property is an independent advisory firm that provides
property valuations, property advisory, and energy advisory services
in the Gippsland/south-east Victoria area. Historically, CJA Lee
Property has predominantly supplied residential property valuation
services to Australian authorised deposit-taking institutions.
C McMahon Enterprises and T.J. Shaw are holding companies of
CJA Lee Property and, apart from CJA Lee Property, do not operate
any other businesses.
HTW Group and CJA Lee Property both supply physical and
electronic property valuation and advisory services.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) has considered the
information provided with the notification waiver application and had
regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Act.
Based on the information provided in the application, including HTW
Group’s revenue in Australia and the cumulative Australian revenue
of CJA Lee Property and from acquisitions in the past three years,
the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely to be
met.
Based on the information provided in the application the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition may raise competition issues. In
particular:
a. there is a material horizontal competitive overlap between
HTW Group and CJA Lee Property in the supply of physical
property valuation and advisory services in the Gippsland
region of Victoria
b. the merger parties’ combined share in the supply of physical
property valuation and advisory services to customers in the
Gippsland region appears to be substantial
c. the extent to which the parties are likely to be constrained by
alternative suppliers of physical property valuation and
advisory services in the Gippsland region is uncertain.
The ACCC considers that these potential competition issues warrant
consideration through the notification process, including via
consultation with third parties.
Given the above, the ACCC has determined not to make the
notification waiver determination applied for.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.
